topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The screen glowed with the familiar, sickening image of the front porch, the peeling paint, the overgrown rose bushes—all of it, meticulously cataloged under a price tag. My knuckles were white where I gripped the phone, the cheap plastic digging into my palm. "No," I whispered, the sound raspy and thin in the quiet living room. I scrolled down, my thumb hovering over the 'Contact Agent' button, a tremor running through my jaw. It wasn't just a house; it was the last tangible piece of the life I thought I had. A surge of hot, tight pressure built behind my eyes, making the room swim. I slammed the phone face down onto the worn wooden table, the resulting thud echoing with a violence that surprised me. I stood up, pacing the cramped space, my breath coming in short, shallow gasps. Every detail of the property listing felt like a deliberate, cruel mockery.

[story 2]

I stared at the listing for what felt like an eternity, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. My chest felt constricted, a heavy, suffocating weight pressing down on my ribs. I reread the address, the street name that had been etched into my memory since I was small. A bitter taste coated the back of my throat. "This isn't real," I muttered, my voice low and vibrating with suppressed intensity. I walked over to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to physically push the feeling out of my system. My hands clenched into fists so tight my nails bit into my skin. The silence of the apartment amplified the frantic drumming inside my own head. I wanted to smash the device, to rip the listing out of the internet and throw it into the nearest abyss.

[story 3]

The notification popped up, stark and undeniable: 'For Sale: 14 Willow Lane.' My stomach twisted into a hard knot, a nauseating churning that made me double over slightly. I couldn't look away. The photos were too perfect, the description too clinical. A low growl escaped my throat, a sound I barely recognized as my own. I began to pace the length of the hallway, my steps sharp and uneven. Each footfall seemed to underscore the sheer unfairness of it all. I stopped abruptly in front of the door, my whole body vibrating with a restless, coiled energy. I didn't move to call anyone; I just stood there, breathing in deep, ragged gulps, the air suddenly too thin to sustain me.
